entry.title: The Next Level of Global Payments: SAP Unlocks Enhanced Cross-Border Capabilities with TransferMate
entry.content:
In an increasingly interconnected global economy, businesses need payment solutions that are fast, transparent, secure, and seamlessly integrated into their ERP systems. That’s why the newly announced partnership between SAP and TransferMate represents a major step forward for finance and treasury teams.

Through this partnership, TransferMate’s cross-border payment service is now integrated directly into SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity as a non-bank payments provider bringing powerful and innovative global payment capabilities straight into SAP ERP solutions, including SAP Cloud ERP.

For organizations operating across multiple currencies and jurisdictions, this integration has the potential to significantly simplify international financial operations.

Traditionally, making international payments required organizations to juggle multiple banking partners, external platforms, or third-party portals. This fragmented processes introduced friction, increased operational risk, and added unnecessary costs that this partnership can solve

What You Can Expect from TransferMate’s integration into SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity

- Fast, transparent and efficient international payments without leaving your ERP

  • No additional portals or manual steps; enjoy seamless straight-through processing.
  • Automated flows from SAP ERP directly to TransferMate payment infrastructure reducing errors, manual intervention, and operational risk.

- Reduced complexity and improved scalability

  • SAP users can send and receive funds globally through a the unified and standardized payment infrastructure from TransferMate.
  • A scalable payment solution that grows with organizations expanding their international footprint.

- Greater transparency and control

  • End-to-end visibility from cross border payment initiation to settlement
  • Enhanced liquidity management with better payment tracking and more payment options.

- Strengthened compliance and security

  • TransferMate operates a fully regulated global payments network, ensuring adherence to local and international financial regulations.

Who Can Benefit?

Any organization using SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity—regardless of whether they are running SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA, or SAP Cloud ERP—can take advantage of this integration.

As companies continue to expand globally, the demand for frictionless, embedded financial tools will only grow. The integration of TransferMate into SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity marks a major step toward making global payments as simple, secure, and seamless as domestic transactions—directly from your SAP environment.

Want to Know More?

SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity

  • https://www.sap.com/germany/products/financial-management/multi-bank-connectivity.html
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_MULTI_BANK_CONNECTIVITY

TransferMate

  • https://www.transfermate.com/
  • TransferMate Press Release

entry.title: IFRS 18 Explained: What SAP S/4HANA Customers Need to Know Before 2027
entry.content:
Spoiler
 

IFRS 18 introduces one of the biggest changes to financial statement presentation in decades, replacing IAS 1 and redefining how companies structure their Profit and Loss statements. This blog breaks down the new categories, required subtotals, MPM rules, and cash-flow changes in simple, human language — and explains how SAP is preparing to support customers across S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, and On-Premise. With comparative reporting for 2026 and mandatory adoption from 2027, now is the time for finance and IT teams to understand what’s coming and how to prepare their SAP landscape.

What Is IFRS 18 and Why Is It Important?

IFRS 18 is a brand-new reporting standard released by the IASB. It replaces IAS 1 (Presentation of Financial Statements) and becomes effective on January 1, 2027.

In simple terms, IFRS 18 changes how companies present their Income Statement (P&L) and how they explain their financial performance.

Think of it as a cleaner, more structured way to show:

  • Where money comes from
  • Where it goes
  • What part of the business truly drives performance

The goal is to:

  • Make financial statements easier to understand
  • Improve consistency across companies
  • Increase transparency for investors
  • Make comparison between companies more fair and simple

What’s Changing in the Profit and Loss Statement? 

IFRS 18 completely reorganizes the Profit and Loss (P&L) structure.
Instead of mixing items together, companies must now classify every income and expense into new categories:

New Mandatory Categories

  • Operating: Day-to-day business activities
  • Investing: Returns or costs related to long-term investments
  • Financing: Borrowings, interest, and funding-related items
  • Income Tax: All tax-related items
  • Discontinued Operations: Business operations that are shut down or sold

New Required Subtotals

Companies must show these subtotals:

  • Operating Profit
  • Profit or Loss Before Financing and Income Tax
  • Profit or Loss

This improves clarity and avoids situations where different companies calculate “operating profit” differently.

What Are Management-Defined Performance Measures (MPMs)?

IFRS 18 introduces strict rules for something many companies already do:
show custom KPIs like “Adjusted EBITDA,” “Core Profit,” or “Underlying Margin.”

These are called Management-Defined Performance Measures (MPMs).

Under IFRS 18, companies MUST:

  • Explain how the measure is calculated
  • Explain why it is useful
  • Provide a reconciliation to an official IFRS subtotal
  • Include this reconciliation in the notes, and it must be audited

This improves trust and transparency around custom metrics that companies use to tell their story.

Changes to the Cash Flow Statement

IFRS 18 also updates IAS 7 (Cash Flow Statements).
Previously, companies had flexibility in classifying certain cash flows.

Under IFRS 18:

  • Dividends paid → must be shown under Investing activities
  • Interest paid → must be shown under Financing activities
  • Interest received → must be shown under Investing activities

This eliminates the old “choice-based” method and brings global consistency.

Impact on Financial Reporting and Disclosure

IFRS 18 affects several areas of financial reporting:

P&L Structure

Companies must reorganize their charts of accounts and reporting hierarchies.

Disclosures and Notes

More detail is required, especially around MPMs, aggregation rules, and reconciliation.

Comparative Reporting

Mandatory disclosure of 2026 figures in the new IFRS 18 structure when reporting for 2027.

What Does This Mean for Companies Using SAP?

SAP is currently analysing the full impact of IFRS 18 and consulting with customers through Customer Engagement Initiatives.

Key areas SAP is reviewing include:

  • Functional areas
  • General Ledger structure
  • Valuation processes
  • Reporting frameworks in S/4HANA
  • Group Reporting layouts
  • Cash-flow classifications
  • Universal Journal (ACDOCA) extensibility

SAP expects to release a formal solution approach by December 2025(as documented in SAP Note-https://me.sap.com/notes/3670330/E) . This guidance is expected to be included in the IFRS 18 updates for SAP Group Reporting as part of the 1SG content package.

The solution may differ across editions:

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
    → Expect more standardized, pre-delivered content
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
    → More flexibility with governance
  • SAP S/4HANA On-Premise
    → Potentially greater configurability

SAP will deliver updates through notes, documentation, and future releases.

How Customers Can Prepare Today

Even before SAP finalizes its solution, companies can begin preparing by:

✔ Reviewing current P&L structures
✔ Identifying where income/expense items will map under IFRS 18
✔ Understanding new disclosure requirements
✔ Reviewing cash-flow classifications
✔ Collecting internal MPM definitions
✔ Engaging with SAP through CEI or Customer Influence Requests

Early preparation will make the transition smoother when IFRS 18 becomes mandatory.

Conclusion

IFRS 18 represents a major shift in how companies communicate their financial performance. The new structure, required subtotals, stricter rules for custom KPIs, and updates to cash flow presentation will significantly impact both finance teams and system configurations.

As outlined in SAP Note 3670330, SAP is developing a comprehensive solution targeted for late 2025, with further updates expected as IFRS 18 continues to evolve. In my view, this is the right time for organizations to start understanding the new requirements, assess their current data and reporting structures, and begin preparing their SAP landscapes for a smooth transition.

More updates will come as SAP refines its roadmap — stay tuned.

entry.title: Direct Debit Advance Notices for Mandates in Contract Accounting (S/4HANA & S/4HANA Cloud Private)
entry.content:
This blog post introduces the procedure of creating an advance notice to inform payers about future direct debit payment collection in SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. It is aim for the usage related to direct debit mandates in Contract Accounting.

If you want to know more about direct debit mandates, see the SAP Help Portal documentation Management of Direct Debit Mandate | SAP Help Portal.

If you want to know more about this topic in SAP S/4HANA Cloud public edition, see the blog post Direct Debit Advance Notices for Mandates in Contract Accounting.

What is Advance Notices?

Before the collection of the future direct debit payments, the payee can send an advance notice to inform the payer about the payment collection.

Prerequisites

Before creating direct debit advance notices, you must complete the following customizing activities:

  1. Setting up the number ranges for advance notices
  2. Activating the advance notice function
  3. Configuring the output of advance notices, including form templates, email templates and form template for correspondence

Setting up the number ranges for advance notices

This activity allows you to set up a range of numbers within which the advance notices are valid.

  1. Open the Enter Number Ranges for Direct Debit Prenotifications using the transaction FPN11.
  2. Create entries of numerical ranges between which the direct debit advance notices are valid.

Activating the Advance Notice Function

In this activity you activate the direct debit advance notice function. Complete the following activations in different steps:

  • Open the Define General Settings activity using  the transaction DDMA_CENTRALSET_FICA.
    Select the checkbox.
  • Open the Define Payment Methods using the transaction FQP1.
    In this activity you activate the direct debit advance notice for the payment method by countries/regions.
    In the Payment Methods under Required master record specification group box, select the Direct Debit Prenotification checkbox.
  • Open Company Code Details for Payment using the transaction FQP4.
    In this activity you activate the direct debit advance notice for the specific company code.
    Select your company code and double click.
    In the Specifications for SEPA Payments/Direct Debit Mandate group box, select the Direct Debit Pre-notification checkbox.

    In this activity you activate the direct debit advance notice for the payment method by countries/regions.
    In the Payment Methods under Required master record specification group box, select the Direct Debit Prenotification checkbox.

Correspondence

The standard correspondence type “0048” is used to generate output and SAP provides pre-defined application form FIN_CA_DDM_ADVANCE_NOTICE for generating the output.

You can re-define the output in the Print Workbench: Application Form transaction (EFRM) to prepare your own application form.

If you redefine your application form, open the Define Application Forms for Correspondence activity (view V_TFK070F in the transaction SM30) to replace the SAP default application form.

You can also implement BAdI FKKCORR_EVENTS_CREATE based on your business requirements.

Procedure of Creating Direct Debit Advance Notices in Contract Accounting

If you activate advance notice for a payment method, you cannot generate payment through the Payment Run transaction (FPY1) for this payment method unless the advance notice for this payment has been created. To create an advance notice for the payment, see the following steps.

  1. Create an advance notice using the transaction FPYP.
    Fill the values in the necessary fields.

    When you schedule a run, you can see the message about direct debit pre-notification with its number is created in the job list.

  2. You can display, change, reverse, or delete the advance notices of mandates using the following transactions:
    1. Display: FPPRN3
    2. Change: FPPRN2
    3. Reverse: FPPRN8
  3. Print the correspondence using the transaction FPCOPARA.
    Fill in the fields in the Run ID and schedule program run. You can refresh the Run Status until you see the Program Run is set to Complete.
    To check the output, you can use Correspondence History transaction (CORRHIST)
    When the Advance notice is generated, you can continue and use transaction FPY1 to generate Payment.
  4. Execute a payment run with advance notices using the transaction FPY1.
    You must select the Pre-Notified Items checkbox to include the open items in payments.

    After you schedule a run, you should see a message - Payment is made in accordance with direct debit pre-notification (num.)

You can ensure that the direct debit is executed only within or after a given number of days after the direct debit advance notices.

Implement your own logic in the event 657 to set Execution Date. Then you can use the option Execution Date To on the selection screen in the Payment Run transaction (FPY1).

DATA: lv_pnexd      TYPE pnexd_kk,
      lv_ddan_calid TYPE tfacd-ident,
      lv_num_days   TYPE i.

  " Only for Advance Notice for Direct Debit Mandate
  IF i_fkkprnh-mndid IS INITIAL OR
     i_fkkprnh-anwnd NE cl_fkkid_ddm_ut=>get_anwnd_for_ddm_fica( ).
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  " Calculate Minimum Number of Days between Notification and Collection
  IF i_process = 'I'.

    lv_num_days    = 10. " set default value for number of days
    lv_ddan_calid  = ''. " set default calendar id

    CALL FUNCTION 'FKK_ADD_WORKINGDAY'
      EXPORTING
        i_date      = sy-datum
        i_days      = lv_num_days
        i_calendar1 = lv_ddan_calid
      IMPORTING
        e_date      = lv_pnexd.
    IF c_fkkprnh_cus-pnexd < lv_pnexd.
      c_fkkprnh_cus-pnexd = lv_pnexd.
    ENDIF.
  ELSEIF i_process = 'U'.
*   nothing to change
  ELSEIF i_process = 'R'.
*   nothing to change
  ENDIF.

 

If the standard selection criteria in the Payment Run transaction (FPY1) are not enough, you can add more rules in the event 610. With this event, you can reject a payment for processing when the Execution Date does not meet the requirements regarding of the number of days between the notification and the payment collection date. 

DATA: ls_messa_kk TYPE messa_kk. 
 
* implementation - Direct Debit Mandates - Advance Notice 
  IF i_para-xprnh NE space. 
    LOOP AT t_0610 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_op>). 
      IF i_dpayc-erdat < <fs_op>-pnexd. 
        " deactivate item 
        <fs_op>-xaktp_0610 = abap_false. 
        " set exception:  Pre-Notification with Unsuitable Date 
        <fs_op>-poken_0610  = '053'. 
        " add info message:  Doc. &1 amt.&2 skipped,execution date of direct debit adv.notice &3 is &4 
        ls_messa_kk-msgid = 'ZZ...'. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgno = 'NNN'. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgty = 'I'. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgv1 = <fs_op>-opbel. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgv2 = <fs_op>-betrw && '/' && <fs_op>-waers. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgv3 = <fs_op>-pnnum. 
        ls_messa_kk-msgv4 = <fs_op>-pnexd+6(2) && '/' && 
                            <fs_op>-pnexd+4(2) && '/' && 
                            <fs_op>-pnexd(4). 
        APPEND ls_messa_kk TO t_messa_kk. 
      ENDIF. 
    ENDLOOP. 
  ENDIF.

You can refer to the following SAP Notes:

  • 3526188 - Direct Debit Mandates in Contract Accounting #4 (Advance Notice for Direct Debit) 
  • 3526296 - Direct Debit Mandates in Contract Accounting #3 (Advance Notice for Direct Debit) 

Is this blog post helpful to you? Leave comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Continuous Influence for SAP Advanced Financial Closing
entry.content:
Dear finance experts,

We’ve got some exciting news! SAP Continuous Influence now has its very own dedicated session for SAP Advanced Financial Closing. This means a much clearer and easier way for you to check out ideas and vote on them, separate from the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Finance session. 

Think of this as your direct line to SAP Product Management. You can submit your own improvement ideas, see what others are suggesting, and even vote or add your comments. Your votes are crucial because they help us prioritize what gets reviewed and potentially added to the backlog. The voting threshold is 8 votes. When voting, please consider which solutions you would like to use productively once available. We’ll keep you updated on the review outcomes in a blog post right within the SAP Advanced Financial Closing session.

When submitting your request, you can choose from the following categories:

  • Task List Creation
  • Task Processing
  • Reporting
  • Configuration and Administration
  • Cross Topics
  • Other

Ready to share your ideas? Go to the session now and start following it to receive updates. If you’re new to Continuous Influence, there’s a helpful Getting Started guide that covers everything from registering to getting notifications. 

Your ideas and suggestions are incredibly valuable for shaping the future of SAP Advanced Financial Closing. We’re looking forward to enhancing the product together with you! 

Your AFC Product Team

 

For more information on SAP Advanced Financial Closing, check out the following links:

    • SAP Community Page
    • SAP Help Portal
    • SAP Roadmap Explorer
    • SAP.com Page

entry.title: Bookmark - How to estimate your costs using Cost Estimator to track your Finops 💰
entry.content:
Why Cost Estimation on SAP BTP Matters

SAP BTP offers flexibility and scalability, but without proper cost visibility, you risk:

  • Budget overruns
  • Unplanned expenses
  • Difficulty aligning IT spend with business goals

Cost estimation ensures you can plan ahead, optimize usage, and stay compliant with financial governance.

How It Supports FinOps

FinOps is about visibility, accountability, and optimization. The Cost Estimator helps by:

  • Providing accurate cost projections for SAP BTP services
  • Allowing scenario planning for scaling workloads
  • Supporting continuous cost monitoring and optimization

Reference :

  • The FinOps Foundation: https://www.finops.org/

What is the SAP BTP Cost Estimator?

The SAP BTP Cost Estimator is a tool that helps you:

  • Simulate service usage across different BTP components
  • Forecast monthly and annual costs based on your planned consumption
  • Compare pricing models (subscription vs. consumption-based)

This tool is essential for FinOps teams, enabling transparency and collaboration between finance and engineering.

SAP BTP FinOps tool

  • 📄 Blog introducing the tool: BTP FinOps: Keeping Track of Your Credits
  • 🚀 Discovery Center Mission to start: Mission 4489
  • 🔧 Github project with source code: SAP-samples: btp-resource-consumption-monitor
  • 🎥 Installation Video step-by-step: YouTube

Past

SAP BTP FinOps webinars

  • Webinar 13 March 2025 (DSAG + SUGEN) [introduction]: 📄 Invitation and 🎥 Recording
  • Webinar 12 March 2025 (SAP BTP Garage) [technical]: 📄 Invitation and 🎥 Recording
  • Webinar 16 December 2024 [introduction]: 🎥 Recording

Steps to Estimate Your SAP BTP Costs

  1. Identify Your Services
    List the SAP BTP services you plan to use (e.g., SAP Integration Suite, SAP HANA Cloud).

  2. Access the SAP BTP Cost Estimator
    Navigate to the SAP BTP Cost Estimator in the SAP Discovery Center.

  3. Configure Your Usage

    • Select your region and environment.
    • Input expected usage metrics (e.g., number of API calls, storage size, compute units).

  4. Review the Estimate
    Analyze projected monthly and yearly costs. Look for optimization opportunities like reserved capacity or bundled services.

  5. Track and Adjust
    Compare actual costs in the SAP BTP Cockpit with your estimates. Use this feedback to refine future forecasts.

List of Cost Estimator

SAP HANA Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator
https://hcsizingestimator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/sap/hana/estimator/index.html 

SAP Cloud Logging Capacity Unit Estimator

https://sap-cloud-logging-estimator.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com
SAP Datasphere Capacity Unit Estimatorhttps://dwc-pricing-calculator-sac-saceu10.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/index.html 
SAP Business Data Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator
https://datasphere-estimator-sac-sacus10.cfapps.us10.hana.ondemand.com/ 
SAP Databricks Sizing Calculatorhttps://www.databricks.com/product/pricing/sap-databricks-pricing-calculator 

SAP Kyma runtime Cost Estimator

https://kyma-project.github.io/price-calculator/ 
SAP AI Core Cost Calculator https://ai-core-calculator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/uimodule/index.html 

SAP Build Code Capacity Unit Calculator

https://build-code-calculator.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/ 

 

SAP Build Runtime Capacity Unit Calculator

https://ap-capacityestimator-live.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/ 

 SAP Build Process Automation, document understanding and task mining add-on by UiPath

https://spa-prod-eu-spa-capacity-unit-estimator.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/ 

All other BTP Services

 SAP BTP Serviceshttps://discovery-center.cloud.sap/estimator 

 

Best Practices for SAP BTP FinOps

  • Run estimates before deploying new services.
  • Include all components—integration, database, networking—to avoid hidden costs.
  • Collaborate with finance teams to align estimates with budget cycles.
  • Leverage SAP Discovery Center missions for guidance on cost optimization.

 

Follow me for more updates - https://profile.sap.com/u/Yogananda

entry.title: Your key to next-level liquidity: Working Capital Management with SAP Taulia
entry.content:
Liquidity is crucial in today’s economic climate. While interest rates are rising and refinancing becomes ever more challenging for companies all sizes, a lack of liquidity can heavily disrupt your entire supply chain. So, it’s time to start thinking about your working capital strategy and SAP Taulia is here to help.

Being one of the key components in SAP S/4HANA Cloud’s treasury portfolio, SAP Taulia helps you to bring your working capital strategy to life. The cashflow orchestration platform is tightly integrated into SAP ERP and SAP Business Network, has access to many funders, delivers AI and analytics capabilities, and supports your sustainability journey as well.

 Curious? Let’s dive into more detail, SAP Taulia consists of two scope items that can be activated independently in your SAP ERP: 7DX (Receivables Financing with SAP Taulia) and 7MG (Payables Financing with SAP Taulia).

Scope item 7DX unlocks receivables financing capabilities in your ERP system. Shortly said, this enables you to unlock cash trapped in your invoices remarkably improving your cash flow.

Scope item 7MG covers the Payables side of your business, offering Virtual Cards, Dynamic Discounting and Supply Chain Finance. Dynamic Discounting basically means that you get a discount from suppliers in exchange for early payments. Supply Chain Finance, works similar, but instead a funder makes the early payment and you repay them later.  Virtual Cards, support you in automating and making your transactions safer and easier.

As of release S/4HANA Public Cloud 2508 7MG: Payables Financing with Taulia is the successor of 4AV: Supplier Finance in S/4HANA. Customers who want to set up Supplier Financing will now need to activate scope item 7MG.

This is of course only the tip of the iceberg. If you want to learn more about SAP Taulia, its value add for your business, customer examples, demos and more, I can sincerely recommend to take a look at the below resources:

  • Free learning journey, for a comprehensive overview
  • Solution overview, for a high level overview of SAP Taulia
  • Technical documentation, for an overview of everything that is available
  • Product Tours, for a sneak peek how the products look in the system
  • Follow SAP Taulia on Linkedin to not miss any new updates

entry.title: Income tax/Direct tax and Tax ledger
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog post takes a sides step from Controlling related topics, and focuses on direct tax, however, we still circle around highlighting new functionalities and comparing functionalities between SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition (at the time of writing the latest SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition release is 2025, while the earlier mentioned is 2508). 

Typically, in S/4HANA projects you focus on Transactional taxes, like, VAT, e-invoicing mandates, and other tax related topics, which are more “urgent” from operative reporting and compliance point of view. However, direct tax should not be overlooked as there are fundamental building blocks, which can help in automating the process, some which are more costly to implement at a later stage (e.g. adding a tax ledger with Asset Accounting integration in Private Cloud Edition), while others doesn’t matter that when they are implemented (e.g. taking  SAP Profitability and performance management into use).

 

Figure 1: Overview of SAP Tax solutions.

 

You might still wonder, why bother with this, we have managed well earlier also, there are not that big difference between local GAAP and income tax?

  • Well, you have items which are handled differently, e.g. pensions, can be deductible in tax, but not in local GAAP, you can have different depreciation rules for tax, you might have certain fixed and temporary difference between local gap and tax. Furthermore, bad debt and lease related postings can have different handling between local GAAP and direct tax. All that leads to the fact that income tax calculation requires different set of information and different rules to calculate your financials.
  • There are also companies that face a need to automate this process, or want further transparency and auditability, which are also drivers for improving direct tax process.

1.     Direct Tax in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition

To support direct tax calculation in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition you can decide whether you want to have a full ledger (Ledger 3) (ie. a standard, non-leading ledger), have an extension ledger for tax or only to have tax depreciation areas for reporting purposes (without a real tax ledger).

  • A) Standard ledger. The standard ledger approach (ledger 3, in figure 2 below) enables you to have direct tax related asset postings (depreciations and specific adjustments) integrated into a separate ledger. Meaning direct integration from Asset accounting into this ledger. A standard ledger allows also integration from other sub-ledgers, like Revenue Accounting and Lease Accounting or Treasury. This ledger will also include all postings that are done with ledger group SPACE, meaning all standard postings (invoices, material movements, normal closing postings, etc.). Additionally, this ledger would include all the other postings that are only valid for direct tax, which enables you to report the direct tax relevant items from one place (as well as for direct tax audits you have also all postings in one place). Database size should also be considered with this approach, as it will increase.
    • This approach requires also efforts, as you need to do closing activities for this ledger, so more efforts are required to maintain it. Adding a full ledger and asset accounting after go-live is possible, however, there are limitations and requires usually more work (further details: Subsequent Implementation of a Further Accounting Principle SAP HELP & 2403248 - FI-AA (new): Availability of RAFABNEW & 430618 - Procedure for setting up new depreciation area), in this scenario you should also review all the subledgers required for the new accounting principle and not only Asset Accounting. Subsequent addition of an accounting principle is not yet possible if Universal Parallel Accounting (UPA) is activated.
  • B) Extension ledger.  Having an extension ledger (e.g. on top of your local ledger (Ledger 2 in below example) enables you to make manual postings into the ledger, to have all direct tax adjustments in one ledger (reported together with the underlying ledger). An extension ledger supports only manual postings to balance sheet and P&L accounts. No postings to reconciliation accounts or other automated postings are supported. This means that no manual postings to subledgers, vendor, customer, asset or material can be done there. An extension ledger can be added after go-live. This approach is not recommended approach, as reporting is very limited, e.g. asset accounting (or any other sub-ledger) cannot read from this ledger.
  • C) No additional ledger. Some customers opt for not having any ledger at all for direct tax and only having a statistical tax depreciation area (to follow and report tax depreciation). This means you need to track pure direct tax related postings and reporting outside your SAP system.

 

 

Figure 2: Ledger options with SAP S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition.

Corporate Income Tax Framework is also available, however, the  country scope is limited and is not planned to be extended, so usually not very commonly discussed.

 

2.     Direct Tax in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition

In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition you can have statistical depreciation areas for tax and an extension ledger, but it is currently not possible to have full ledger for tax. This means options B and C from previous chapter are valid approaches.

  • B) Extension ledger. Having an extension ledger (on top of your local ledger (Ledger 2, in below example) enables you to make manual postings into the ledger, to have all direct tax adjustments in one ledger (reported together with the underlying ledger). An extension ledger supports only manual postings to balance sheet and P&L accounts. No postings to reconciliation accounts or other automated postings are supported. This means that no manual postings to subledgers, vendor, customer, asset or material can be done there.
    • An extension ledger can be added after go-live. This approach is not recommended approach, as reporting is very limited, e.g. asset accounting (or any other sub-ledger) cannot read from this ledger.
  • C) No additional ledger. Some customers opt for not having any ledger at all for direct tax and only having a statistical tax depreciation area (to follow and report tax depreciation). This means you need to track pure direct tax related postings and reporting outside your SAP system. SAP pre-delivers per country depreciation areas, which typically includes also a tax depreciation area, but there are country specific differences.
    • Note! Currently only one customer specific (statistical) depreciation area(area 16)  can be added (for local GAAP), during initial implementation, not after go-live (scope item 5HG). So, if you need an additional depreciation area, it can only be added before go-live (no asset postings can exist when you add it). In some cases, you might also have requirements for an additional depreciation area for insurance follow-up (not direct tax). This means it is extra important to check you depreciation areas in the implementation phase. 

 

 

Figure 3: Ledger options in SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud Edition.

The Corporate Income Tax Framework , is also available, but also with limited country scope and is not planned to be extended, so usually not very commonly discussed.

 

3.     Profitability and Performance Management, taking automation to the next level

So far, we have discussed ledgers and asset accounting, but what about all the other posting differences, how to automate them? This is where SAP Profitability & Performance management (PaPM) comes into picture.

PaPM is a strong calculation engine, which was designed to be maintained by business users and not require too much IT support. It has multiple use cases, one of which is corporate income tax. It brings automation, transparency, auditability and process overview to the direct income tax process.  PaPM enables also AI-assisted tax analysis and insights through integration with the Joule copilot.

Using the underlying data discussed in previous chapters, you can complete the remaining calculations and adjustments in PaPM. This enables to have a systematic and rule-based approach for temporary and permanent adjustment, as well as other adjustments. Optionally, PaPM can also make postings back to the tax ledger (or extension ledger (within its limitations)) (both for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition and SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition), for a single source of truth of direct tax postings. Technically PaPM can integrate to any source (also non-SAP), so you can also write back data to SAP ECC.

 

Figure 4: PaPM example with integration to Full tax ledger, together with PaPm and S/4 HANA Private Cloud edition.

To speed up a PaPM implementation you can use the SAP created sample content (SAP PaPM Sample Content All), for further information on the  SAP PaPM Tax Sample content, click here.  In addition to SAP sample content, there are multiple tax partners who create and maintain even more detailed sample content.

 

Figure 5: Overview of Corporate income tax with SAP.

 

It is worth noticing that PaPm comes with multiples other use cases also, BEPS pillar 2, Transfer pricing, Sustainability, Agile planning & Simulations, Activity based costing, etc.

 

Figure 6: Overview of PaPm use cases.

 

4.     Summary

This blog covered different options on how to handle direct tax, whether you only have partial support for direct tax in S/4HANA e.g. from statistical tax depreciation areas in asset accounting or having a full tax ledger, with PaPM you can gain further automation, auditability and process transparency. There are different options, depending on what type of setup suites your organization.  

 

Figure 7: Comparison Table: Direct Tax Management in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud, Public Cloud, and PaPM.

 

  • How automated is your direct tax processes, are you fully automated and auditable or are you using excel?

 

Best Regards,

Dick Westman

entry.title: Argentina Withholding Tax: Understanding the General Resolutions 5762/2025 and 5764/2025
entry.content:
Business Context

The Argentinian tax authority, ARCA, published General Resolution 5762/2025 and General Resolution 5764/2025.

These resolutions, known locally as Resoluciones Generales (RG), repeal the previous RG 1575/2003 and replace the "M" letter documents with "A" letter documents, which now feature specific descriptions:

  • “OPERACIÓN SUJETA A RETENCIÓN” (Transaction Subject to Withholding Tax)
  • “PAGO EN CBU INFORMADA” (Payment with Unique Bank Key)

Extra information: The “M” billing requirement targeted newly operating companies, those with incomplete tax compliance, or insufficient patrimonial solvency.

Who is it Relevant For?

The changes primarily impact VAT-registered taxpayers ("Responsables Inscriptos en IVA"). ARCA will evaluate these companies every four months to determine if they can be treated as normal taxpayers, potentially removing the descriptions from their documents.

Document Codes

Following these changes, the document codes are now organized depending on the descriptions featured:

1. Operación Sujeta a Retención (Transaction Subject to Withholding Tax)

051: Invoice “A”

052: Debit Note “A”

053: Credit Note “A”

2. Pago en CBU Informada (Payment with Unique Bank Key)

001: Invoice “A”

002: Debit Note “A”

003: Credit Note “A”

Final Considerations

Regarding withholding taxes, payments to letter “A” documents with the description “Operación Sujeta a Retención” must be calculated as follows:

  • VAT: 100% over the VAT of the paid invoice, SIRE code 499
  • Earning tax: 6% over the payment (net of taxes), SICORE code 99

Businesses using SAP ECC or SAP S/4HANA can customize their systems to comply with these new requirements. For practical guidance on how to meet this legal requirement in your SAP system, refer to SAP Note 3696508.

These changes aim to streamline tax compliance and ensure transparency in business transactions. As businesses adapt, staying informed and prepared will be key to a smooth transition.

For more details on this legal change, check the regulatory change manager tool under Changes: New Class A Invoice Categories and Withholding Regime.

Thank you for taking the time to read and until next time,

Juliana Ghisleni

entry.title: IFRS 18 Is Coming: What Finance Teams Need to Know TODAY
entry.content:
IFRS 18, the new international reporting standard, is on the horizon—and it’s set to reshape how companies present financial performance. Replacing IAS 1, the new standard introduces clearer categories in the income statement, greater transparency around management-defined performance measures, and more consistency across global reporting.

For finance leaders, this isn’t just an accounting change. It’s a strategic shift requiring updates to reporting processes, systems, internal controls, and guidance for business stakeholders. With implementation deadlines approaching, now is the time to assess readiness and begin planning the transition. The companies that prepare early will benefit from stronger insights and more credible financial communication.

Join the webinar series offered by SAP Experts: "Preparing for IFRS18: what changes and what it means for you" - running in February and March 2026 - Register here at: https://event.on24.com/wcc/r/5207684/12E54196E7A96D46F347624B897ACF23/6546614

entry.title: Unlocking SAP Treasury - TRM - FAQ - Treasury - SAP Treasury Operations
entry.content:
Unlocking the Value of SAP TRM for Corporate Treasury

SAP Treasury and Risk Management (TRM) supports the complete end‑to‑end lifecycle of financial transactions between a corporation and its banking partners. This includes the full processing of instruments such as loans, deposits, FX deals, derivatives, securities, and more. For each transaction, the system automatically generates detailed cashflow schedules and posts all related accounting entries, including principal movements, interest accruals, repayments, fees, and bank charges.

Month‑end activities are fully integrated, covering current‑portion reclassification, amortization, accrual/deferral, foreign‑currency valuation, and mark‑to‑market or fair‑value measurement in accordance with IFRS and local GAAP requirements.

TRM includes more than 120 contract types, reflecting the breadth of financial instruments used by corporate treasuries worldwide. These processes fall under Transaction Management and are complemented by powerful analytical components such as Credit Risk Analyzer, Market Risk Analyzer, Portfolio Analyzer, and Market Data Management. Together, they provide comprehensive exposure monitoring, valuation, risk assessment, and performance analysis across the entire treasury portfolio. Master Data Tricks

1.    Treasury and Risk management vs Corporate Treasury requirement

There is a common misconception that purchasing a license for SAP Treasury and Risk Management (TRM) will cover the entire scope of a company’s treasury operations. In reality, SAP treasury functionality is distributed across multiple modules, and TRM alone does not fulfill all requirements.

Treasury and Risk Management (TRM): Financial instruments, risk management, valuations, and accounting.

Cash and Liquidity Management (CLM): Cash position, liquidity forecasting, and bank account management.

Payment and Bank Communication (BCM): Payment processing, approvals, and bank connectivity.

A complete treasury solution requires all three modules working together. The link below provides additional detail on this topic.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/4785100c7f20412f95174592eff35939/7bc4a1572cfbb576e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

2.    SAP best Practice – Learn TRM Processes

SAP Best Practices are the most effective starting point for learning TRM processes, especially for new users. They provide complete, sequential process flows for all commonly used Treasury functions. Every user and junior consultant who wants to build a strong foundation in the TRM module should begin by exploring the SAP Best Practice scenarios, as they offer structured guidance, standard process steps, and end‑to‑end examples aligned with SAP’s recommended approach.

 

 

3.    Monthly/Periodic Closing – Valuation posting

From an accounting perspective, Treasury operations require multiple financial postings that impact both the Balance Sheet and the Profit & Loss statement. These include interest accruals, FX valuations, bond amortization, current‑portion reclassification, and other postings depending on the organization’s requirements and the contract types in use.

In SAP TRM, all relevant data is captured at the time of deal creation. Based on this information, the system generates structural flows, which serve as the foundation for all valuation and closing activities. Month‑end and periodic closing postings derive their values directly from these structural flows. To execute these processes, transactions such as TPM44, TPM1, TPM18, and others are run as part of the closing cycle. Common valuation and closing processes include:

- Amortization (linear or effective interest method): Posts the positive or negative difference between the purchase value and the face value over the life of the instrument.

- Price Valuation (Mark‑to‑Market): Adjusts the book value based on current market prices, resulting in unrealized gains or losses.

- Security Valuation: Based on yield curves and clean/dirty price calculations for securities.

- Foreign Currency Valuation: Posts FX gains or losses for contracts denominated in foreign currencies.

- Rate Valuation for Forward Contracts: Values forward points and forward premiums/discounts for FX forwards.

- Impairment Procedures: Posts impairment losses and reversals based on valuation rules and credit assessments.

- Current Portion Reclassification: Moves the principal amount of long‑term loans from non‑current to current liabilities in the Balance Sheet.

- Interest Accruals: Posts periodic accruals based on interest amounts for the relevant period.

These are the most commonly used processes, but SAP TRM supports many additional valuation and posting functions depending on organizational policies and the financial instruments in use

 

4.    Link Contracts – References

Sometimes it is necessary to link two separate contracts for reporting or other business requirements. In such cases, the Create Reference (TBRL) option can be used. For example, if a loan needs to be linked with a guarantee, you can create a reference between the two contracts. This linkage can then be utilized for reporting, audit tracking, and overall transparency.

 

5.    Copy Contract

Copy Contract is an easy and efficient way to create a new contract when most of the fields are similar to an existing one. You can access this function through the Collective Processing app or the relevant transaction code.

Simply select the source transaction and choose the Copy option, as shown in the screenshot. The system will create a duplicate of the contract, and you can adjust any fields as needed before saving it

 

 

6.    Reversal

Reversing a contract, activity, or flows can sometimes be a complex process because each contract may have multiple activities and flows posted at different points in time. This often means that multiple reversals must be performed in the correct sequence.

For example, if a reference interest rate has been assigned and an interest flow has already been posted, the system will not allow you to directly reverse the activity. You must follow the same logical order in reverse that you used during the original processing: first reverse the financial posting and the flow, then reverse the assignment of the reference rate, and only after that can you reverse the activity or the entire transaction.

So be careful—if the sequence is not respected, inconsistencies may occur. There are detailed blogs and documentation available on this topic, so it is strongly recommended to understand the end‑to‑end process before performing reversals.

 

7.    Attach Document

The document attachment feature is available for every contract, whether during settlement or while making changes. You can use it to upload supporting documents such as PDFs, Word files, and other relevant materials. When working with banks, there are always important documents that should be attached for future reference and audit purposes.

In the Trade Finance area, a dedicated section is provided specifically for process‑related attachments. This ensures that all documentation linked to letters of credit, guarantees, collections, and other trade finance processes is stored in a structured and easily accessible manner.

 

 

8.    Market Data Provider

We can bring market data into SAP TRM—such as interest rates, forex rates, yield curves, and more. However, this data is not free and is not included with the SAP TRM module. A subscription from a market data provider like Refinitiv, Bloomberg, or others is required.

Your organization needs a corporate license, which can then be integrated with the ERP system. The provider will guide you through the setup once you contact them. After the subscription is active, the integration allows market data to flow automatically into SAP TRM

 

9.    Before Integration with Bank/Financial institute

In today’s environment, Corporate Treasury functions are becoming increasingly demanding, and organizations expect a higher level of automation across all processes. Treasury teams work with multiple external institutions, and these institutions often expect system‑to‑system automation so tasks can be executed without manual intervention. In many cases, they do not want to enter the same information in both SAP and the external institution’s system — which is a completely valid expectation.

SAP is fully capable of integrating with external systems, but before committing to any integration, two key points must be verified: The external institution must actually offer integration services (e.g., data exchange, automated processing, connectivity). The integration method must be clearly defined, such as API, file-based interface, host-to-host, or any other supported mechanism.

If both conditions are met, then integration is technically feasible.

Payments and market data integrations are already common, and many organizations are adopting standardized solutions. However, beyond these typical scenarios, there are other possibilities. For example, SWIFT provides multiple integration options between corporates and banks, even when a bank does not offer a direct API.

Therefore, whenever you plan to work on any integration, ensure that: The external party agrees to support the integration. The integration method is clearly defined and technically supported by both sides.

10.                   Integration Inbound/Outbound

SAP TRM provides extensive integration capabilities both within the organization and with external financial institutions. One of the most common integrations is for market data, where organizations subscribe to a market data provider. Through SAP BTP, this data is automatically transferred into the relevant S/4HANA tables, ensuring accurate and up‑to‑date pricing, yield curves, FX rates, and other valuation inputs.

For payment processing and message exchange, the Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) framework is typically used. MBC enables secure, automated communication between SAP and connected banks.

SAP TRM can also integrate directly with SWIFT, allowing the exchange of financial messages between the corporate system and banks. This requires defining the appropriate inbound and outbound structures to support message formats such as MT and MX.

Similarly, information can be exchanged between S/4HANA and TRM in both directions, depending on the business requirement. The integration possibilities are highly flexible, and SAP places no inherent limitation on how data can flow across systems, provided the structures and interfaces are properly defined

11.                   APIs for Treasury and Risk Management

APIs are available in SAP for retrieving the financial transaction net value, treasury posting journal line items, and position flows. These APIs enable seamless integration with external systems and support automated reporting, reconciliation, and analytics. Further details can be accessed from the link provided below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/d9c0dd5023e34f0d85ed30fc859496aa/325f953a5fb54319921538882b015b5a.html?locale=en-US

 

12.                   Risk Free Rate - SOFR / €STR / SONIA

With the move from old benchmark rates like LIBOR to new Risk‑Free Rates such as SOFR, interest benchmarks have changed for both new and existing loans. In SAP TRM, the solution is now mature and easy to use.

You create the deal using the new RFR interest condition. Only a small configuration change is needed, and then you can select SOFR or any other RFR directly. Old loans can be shifted from LIBOR to SOFR by updating the interest condition in the same deal. SAP handles the transition smoothly without recreating the loan. This works for both Money Market and Securities products. Blogs are available for step by step guide.

 

13.                   Display Change Document

Changes made to a contract can be monitored using the Display Change Document functionality. This feature is essential for internal analysis and audit requirements. SAP records every modification, including the old value, the new value, and the user who performed the change. This ensures full transparency and traceability throughout the contract lifecycle.

 

 

 

 

 

14.                   FTR_TRD_ADJUST Contract Correction

Sometimes flows are not generated in all relevant tables, which makes it impossible to reverse the contract or remove the flows. In such cases, FTR_TRD_ADJUST can be used to correct inconsistencies between the tables. This issue is rare, but the transaction is very useful when a correction is needed.

 

15.                   IFRS18 – Starting 2027

IFRS 18 is a new reporting standard issued by the IASB that will replace IAS 1 – Presentation of Financial Statements starting January 1, 2027. It introduces a more structured and consistent approach to presenting financial performance, with the goal of enhancing comparability, transparency, and usefulness of financial statements across entities and industries.

Entities must present income and expenses using defined, standardized categories, reducing diversity in practice and making performance reporting more comparable.

Below Link is further help you to implement in SAP

3670330 - Financial Reporting according to IFRS 18 in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA - SAP for Me

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/ifrs-18-compliance-for-treasury-and-risk-management-in-sap-cloud-erp-key/ba-p/14299973

 

 

16.                   Collective processing apps

Collective Processing apps provide a far more efficient way to handle activities within specific treasury areas such as Money Market or Trade Finance. They not only allow users to execute multiple processing options from a single place, but they also function like reports, giving visibility into all relevant transactions before taking action.

In my experience, these apps are highly recommended compared to using individual transactions like FTR_CREATE or FTR_EDIT. They offer greater flexibility, a clearer overview of available functions, and a more intuitive way to manage large volumes of deals or operational tasks.

 

 

 

17.                   Additional Account Assignment e.g Cost centre/Profit Centre

Additional account assignments such as Cost Center or Profit Center must be maintained directly in the TRM configuration under the Accounting section. This is done through “Allocate Additional Account Assignment to Account Assignment Reference” or “Additional Account Assignment”, where you also define the appropriate derivation rules. These settings ensure that the correct account assignment objects are automatically derived during contract creation and later during financial postings.

It is important not to assign these objects through OKB9 or by setting a default Profit Center, as this can cause inconsistencies and conflicts with other modules. This will be applied during financial postings such as TBB1.

 

18.                   Additional Fields/Tab in Financial instrument (Contract)

You can add up to two additional tabs in the financial transaction screen, and each tab can contain multiple fields. The design depends entirely on the business requirement—whether you need simple input fields, a table where users can add multiple rows, or fields that integrate with existing calculations or missing processes.

I’ve used this enhancement in many projects, and it’s extremely useful. However, it’s important to understand its limitations. The additional tabs do appear for every financial instrument. Since SAP only allows a maximum of two custom tabs, they must be designed carefully. If the requirement goes beyond what two tabs can handle, it’s better to introduce segments to structure the information more efficiently

BADI:  FTR_CUSTOMER_EXTENT

SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction Manager>Transaction Management>Enhancement

 

19.                   Calander or Date Check

Before concluding a financial transaction, SAP Treasury and Risk Management provides a Date Check function to ensure that key dates—such as due dates, fixing dates, or settlement dates—fall on valid working days. This validation is performed against the working calendars assigned to the company code or currency.

If the entered or system‑calculated date is not a working day (for example, a weekend or a public holiday), the system automatically displays the previous and next available working days. This helps users select an appropriate date and prevents downstream settlement or processing issues.

During transaction creation or settlement, if a date does not comply with any of these calendars, SAP triggers a popup warning, prompting the user to adjust the date accordingly.

This functionality ensures operational accuracy, reduces settlement failures, and aligns all transaction dates with market and counterparty conventions.

 

20.                   Tables in SAP TRM

Finding the correct table in SAP Treasury & Risk Management (TRM) can be confusing because financial transactions span multiple layers: header, activity, flows, conditions, and accounting. SAP provides a very efficient way to discover exactly where data is stored.

FTR_SHOW is the most reliable tool to identify all tables linked to a specific financial transaction. Open FTR_SHOW, Enter Company Code, Enter the Transaction Number, Execute You will see: All tables used for that transaction

If you need to explore beyond the basics, SAP naming conventions help a lot. Useful patterns VTB* → Treasury transaction tables, VTI* → Market data / valuation tables, TLCT* → Condition and lifecycle-related tables. These prefixes quickly guide you to the right technical area.

When you know a field but not the table: Go to SE11 Enter the View (e.g., V_TVBFHA, V_TVBFHAPO), Select the field, Use Where-Used List. This is extremely useful when debugging, enhancing, or writing custom reports.

21.                   CDS view in SAP TRM

CDS query views can be consumed by any reporting tool that supports CDS-based analytics, including SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office, Design Studio, SAP Lumira, and SAP Analytics Cloud. These views provide cleaner, business‑ready data compared to raw tables, making them ideal for developing analytical reports and dashboards. The link below provides the complete list of CDS views available in the TRM module.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/848f8ce21bcd4f67bce77494799e2257/f71f1f5c39374159bb9f07cbc0e05aa2.html?locale=en-US

 

22.                   Financial instrument vs Process

Understanding which processes (Change, Settle, Rollover, etc.) apply to which financial instrument is essential for correct transaction handling in SAP Treasury and Risk Management.

SAP provides a simple way to check which processes are valid for a specific financial instrument using FTR_EDIT. On the right-hand side, select the Financial Instrument (e.g., Money Market, FX, Securities, Derivatives). Observe the left-hand side buttons (Change, Settle, Rollover, Reverse, etc.).

Pressable (active) buttons → These processes are allowed for the selected financial instrument.

Greyed-out (inactive) buttons → These processes are not applicable for that instrument.

Each financial instrument type has its own lifecycle and permitted actions.

 

 

23.                   Product Type Configuration

All product types come preconfigured in the system, and based on business requirements, we can adjust the configuration or create new product types as needed. A product type includes several dependent configuration elements such as Transaction Types, Flow Types, Conditions, and other related settings.

When defining a new product type, all these components must be configured in the correct sequence to ensure consistency and proper system behaviour. One of the easiest and most reliable approaches is to use the Copy tool. It automatically copies all dependent configuration objects along with the product type, which significantly simplifies the setup process and reduces the risk of missing any required configuration.

If you want, I can also expand this into a step‑by‑step guide or a configuration checklist.SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction management>General Settings>Tools>Copy or rename a product type and dependent settings

 

24.                   TBB1 vs TBB1_LC

TBB1 is used to post financial documents for active treasury flows. During migration, however, TBB1_LC is used to post legacy flows. This marks the flows as posted in Treasury without generating any FI documents. For example, if a loan was taken two years ago with a five‑year tenor and we are now moving to TRM, the full amortization schedule will be created in the system. The historical two years of flows will be posted using TBB1_LC to avoid duplicate accounting, while all future flows will be posted through TBB1.

25.                   Financial Document Enhancement

It is possible to capture the required information in financial document fields such as Assignment, Header Text, or Reference. These fields are later utilized by the Finance department for reporting and bank‑clearing activities. This approach also enables updating/replacing the input across multiple fields as needed.

SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction manager> Accounting>Link to other Accounting components> Enhancement in Posting interface

Definition name: TPM_ACCIF_TRAC

26.                   AAR Account Assignment Reference

AAR is used to derive the GL account during financial document posting, and it is assigned to each position at the time of deal creation. Its value is determined through the derivation logic maintained in the Accounting configuration. Although it is technically possible to manually change or correct this field during deal creation or editing, doing so is not recommended. If an adjustment is required, the AAR can be replaced using transaction TPM28.

 

27.                   Hedge management Net Open Exposure vs Balance Sheet Hedging

Net Open Exposure relies on TRM Exposure Management and Cash Management as its primary sources of exposure data. In contrast, Balance Sheet Hedging derives its exposure from FI Balances, FI Open Items, and Cash Management.

28.                   Roles in Treasury and Risk Management

Below are the standard SAP Fiori roles. Assign these roles to the user, and the corresponding Fiori apps will become visible in their launchpad.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_RISK_MANAGER: Leads the treasury department. Evaluates financial statuses and performs overall reporting. Manages traders and their authorizations.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_FOE: Concludes and manages financial transactions to hedge foreign exchange and interest rate risks.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_MOE: Monitors financial risks, such as credit risks and market risks. Is responsible for mitigation of risks in compliance with the corporate treasury guidelines (hedging policy) and ensures conformity with accounting regulations.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_BOE: Processes financial transactions, manages correspondence and sends confirmations to counterparties, imports market data, and calculates net present values.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_ACCOUNTANT: Processes and valuates the treasury positions and makes the relevant postings in Financial Accounting to ensure the accurate portrayal of treasury activities.

29.                   Document Management System for TRM

DMS must be configured correctly to enable the generation of correspondence messages. SAP Note 1320374 provides the detailed steps required for this configuration

30.                   Treasury Analytics

Analytics is a critical requirement for our system landscape, and one of the most valuable components is the Treasury Executive Dashboard delivered as example content within SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). This dashboard leverages the standard CDS query views available for Treasury Management in the back-end system, providing real‑time insights and actionable intelligence.

Treasury Alerts play a key role in ensuring timely execution of process steps. These alerts support various activities such as correspondence, settlement, release, interest rate updates, postings, and payments—helping teams stay compliant and responsive.

The Debt and Maturity Analysis and Debt and Maturity Profile reports offer detailed visibility into debt positions over time, along with powerful drill‑down capabilities for deeper analysis.

The Market Data Overview provides a consolidated view of financial risks related to foreign exchange (FX) and market data. Users can monitor current and historical FX spot rates, FX swap rates, reference interest rates, security prices, and other key indicators for a selected date.

The Foreign Exchange Overview further enhances risk transparency by summarizing FX‑related exposures, including FX instruments, financial status, cash position, and liquidity forecasts.

Beyond these, a wide range of additional Fiori‑based Treasury reports are available. With the flexibility of CDS views, organizations can also design and build their own SAC stories tailored to specific Treasury requirements

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog.
If you notice anything that needs correction, please feel free to let me know.


Also, tell me if there’s any topic you’d like me to add, or if you’d prefer a step‑by‑step explanation of any section.


If you found this helpful, please share it so others can benefit as well

entry.title: FI-AA: Possible Issues Post S/4HANA Transition
entry.content:
This blog is to share a few things that were experienced in a S/4HANA - post Project transition and the way it was resolved, successfully. Might be helpful for someone encountering a similar issue.

Fixed Assets:

Once the S/4HANA migration is fully completed say from ECC and later when a new Company Code is directly created in S/4H environ, the Asset module requires some adjustments.

Assuming there is a Chart of Depreciation already existing for a specific Country / Company Code and the new Company Code also requires to be assigned to the existing COD, system does not allow the same. Despite the fact that multiple Company Codes can be assigned to a single Chart of Depreciation, as per standard model.

We might face errors in multiple transaction codes like 'FINSC_LEDGER', 'FAA_CMP' etc. (owing to currency related settings).

Therefore, to overcome this scenario, SAP recommends creating a new Chart of Depreciation and assign the new Comp Code to the same. In addition, a few more settings are also required, as given below.

Check and update transaction Codes 'OADB','OADC' and under SPRO: Asset Accounting > General Valuation > Currencies > Specify the Use of Parallel Currencies

The Screen Prints are just for representation purposes only 😊.

In this regard, please find a link to SAP OSS Note - https://me.sap.com/notes/2882574/E (2882574 - Message ACC_AA116 for a new company code created in SAP S/4HANA)

The note clearly states "currently, there is no other solution available in FI-AA".

Balance C/F - FAGL-GVTR: 

Another error could be encountered during Balance carry forward. This could be due to a missed sequence in close process, by a user. Please find link to SAP OSS Note - https://me.sap.com/notes/3630930 (3630930 - Error in transaction FAGLGVTR although the fiscal year was successful carried forwarded in asset accounting) The note describes further:

Quote: " error message AA594 is issued for each processed asset in the balance carryforward (Errors occurred for asset 1780 60603514-0 in fiscal year 2026). This error message is the information that postprocessing activities (-> AFAR) are necessary.

Nevertheless, at the end, you receive the information:
CCode XXXX Fiscal year change 2026 performed, postprocessing necessary." Unquote.

The note is pretty useful and guides well.

Depending on the Product and support pack level, there are more notes available to guide on the BCF related issues. Reproducing below, excerpts from one common note; link to OSS.

Hope you find the information useful. Thanks for your comments and feedback.

To view my other blogs, please follow the link.

Regards,
J. Swaminathan

entry.title: New SAP Learning Insights podcast episode with Stefanos’ Finance Update is live now!
entry.content:
In this episode, David Chaviano met again with Stefanos Pougkas from SAP to talk about the evolution of SAP's financial offerings. They explore the core functions of SAP Financials, the shift to SAP Public Cloud solutions, and the importance of foundational accounting knowledge for newcomers. Stefanos highlights the impact of AI on automating financial processes and encourages embracing a fit-to-standard philosophy in cloud environments. We conclude by discussing the future of finance consultants amidst evolving market demands, offering valuable insights for anyone looking to excel in SAP finance. And of course, at the end of the episode, Stefanos shares some words of wisdom.

entry.title: Understanding the New Tags in the SAF-T Poland VAT Report (JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3))
entry.content:
Poland's tax reporting landscape is evolving, and SAP has introduced new tags in the Standard Audit File for Tax (SAF-T) structures JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) to help businesses stay compliant with the country's electronic invoicingrequirements. 

Understanding the Four New Tags 

In this blog article, we' ll explain what the new tags mean and how to use them, along with examples to make things clearer. 

The new structures JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) of the Polish SAF-T VAT report now include the following tags designed to simplify the reporting process: 

  • "NrKSeF": This tag is the KSeF Reference Number, which serves as an identifier for invoices in the National e-Invoice System (KSeF).  
  • "OFF": Use this tag for invoices referred to in Article 106nf(1) of the act that don't have a KSeF identification number and haven't been submitted to KSeF.  
  • "BFK": This applies to either electronic or paper invoices not categorized as e-invoices by KSeF, for example in cases where it’s not mandatory to issue invoices using the National e-Invoice System.  
  • "DI": Represents any proof of transaction other than an invoice issued through the KSeF. This applies to all other cases of documents that will not have a KSeF number, have not been sent to KSeF and can’t be categorized as ‘BFK’ or ‘OFF’.

Examples 

To help companies meet the new standards, let's look at examples of where these tags are applicable. 

The ‘NrKSeF’ Tag 

Use the "NrKSeF" tag when there's a KSeF Reference Number for an invoice. This number identifies the invoice within the National e-Invoice System. For example, if you're processing an invoice registered in KSeF, you should tag it with the appropriate "NrKSeF" number. 

Complete this field if, as of the date of submission of JPK_VAT, the invoice has been assigned a KSeF number. 

The ‘OFF’ Tag 

Apply the "OFF" tag to invoices referred to in Article 106nf(1) of the act that don’t have a KSeF number as of the date of submission of the records.  

This designation applies to invoices issued during the period of malfunction of the National e-Invoice System, according to Art.106nf(1). This designation indicates that, due to the inactivity of this system, the invoice does not have a KSeF identifying number as of the date of submission of the JPK_VAT. 

If an invoice exists in e-Document processing but still lacks a KSeF Reference Number, mark it as "OFF". 

The ‘BFK’ Tag 

The "BFK" tag is for invoices not handled by KSeF, such as paper invoices, vendor invoices for foreign transactions or invoices with document types that are excluded from KSeF. This category also includes invoices from cash registers, invoices from consumers in B2C relations issued outside of KSeF, invoices for financial services using KSeF exemption, etc.  

This tag allows you to indicate transactions that for various legal reasons fall outside of the KSeF.  

If the document is not marked with DI, OFF or KSeF th en it is reported by JPK_V7K(3) and JPK_V7M(3) with status BFK.

The ‘DI’ Tag 

Use the "DI" tag for all other documents that serve as transaction proofs but are not an invoice and do not qualify as ‘OFF’ or ‘BFK’.  

For example, daily reports from cash registers, internal documents, customs documents, or other accounting documents.  

Invoices issued in the offline24 mode must also be tagged with the ‘DI’ tag.  

DI should be also assigned to invoices, which should be registered in KSeF, but due to different reasons cannot be registered. This may happen in the initial period applicable with KSeF, when for various reasons, mainly technological and configuration ones, the taxpayer is unable to register the invoice in KSeF, but will have to forward it to the business partner. 

With SAP Document and Reporting Compliance you can select for which type of transaction DI is relevant. See the example below. 

Why This Matters

These tags help you accurately categorize your transactions, ensuring compliance with Polish tax regulations while reducing reporting errors.  

You can also find detailed information about the changes in the JPK_V7M(3) and JPK_V7K(3) structures in the Regulatory Change Manager. 

For detailed guidance on implementing KSeF requirements in your SAP system, check out the related blog articles. 

  • How Official Document Numbering helps to fulfill the Polish KSeF Requirements in S4HANA Public Cloud 
  • How you can manage Polish KSeF Requirement in SAP S/⁠4HANA Cloud Private Edition & SAP S/⁠4HANA 
  • How to store long Supplier Invoice Reference Numbers in SAP S/4HANA ERP Products 

 Staying updated with the latest legal changes is essential for navigating the ever-evolving taxation landscape. By understanding how to use these tags, you can ensure compliance with national regulations, reduce errors, and streamline your tax processes.

entry.title: Paying with stablecoins in B2B scenarios - business process end-to-end
entry.content:
As an SAP S/4HANA user, you can pay your suppliers with stablecoins. Or receive payments in stablecoins. Key to it is SAP Digital Currency Hub. Here's a process-focused perspective on it.

Paying Supplier Invoices

Assuming your company is using SAP Digital Currency Hub, it's as easy to pay with stablecoins as paying the way you are used to: as an accounts payable accountant, you do a payment run. A dedicated payment method (you could use D for digital) would find all supplier invoices for business partners accepting stablecoins.

Payment Run in S/4HANA

Once the payment is released, it is sent via SAP's Multi-Bank Connector to the SAP Digital Currency Hub (DCH), which initiates payment immediately. It interacts with your custody provider, to process the stablecoin payment, and confirms execution of the payment, allowing to clear the payable in your accounts.

SAP Digital Currency Hub - Outgoing Payment

SAP Digital Currency Hub - Blockchain Transaction

Additionally, when doing the payment run, you should send a remittance advice from your ERP system, using the established ways like email or direct integrations, to the other party, detailing the payment's purpose.

Receiving Payment for Receivables

Assuming, you have communicated a dedicated destination address to your customer, they will direct their payment to it. SAP Digital Currency Hub communicates permanently with your custody provider to check for incoming payments on the blockchain networks. Once a payment is received, the address used tells, who has paid. Together with the payment date and amount, the customer's ID is reported back to your S/4HANA as an incoming payment.

As the currently used stablecoin format definitions (ERC-20) do not support fields like "note to payee" or "payment reference", a remittance advice, received on an established channel (see above), will help to reconcile this payment with an open receivable.

Destination addresses are a great way to receive payments. They can be seen as an alias for your wallet's address, generated only for a certain business partner. Hence, all payments received on this destination address can be attributed to them - hence only they know it.

Payment preparation in SAP Digital Currency Hub

As an accountant, you usually do not enter the SAP Digital Currency Hub, which is intended to run as a rather invisible component. Nevertheless, to establish the payment agreement for a business partner, you need to log in.

The payment agreement consists of a set of destination addresses.

  1. Receive-to addresses: For your customer, you will create a dedicated address they have to pay to. Payments received on this address will be attributed to this business partner. So, you need to communicate it to them.
  2. Pay-to addresses: As a customer, you will enter the destination address received from your supplier. You will direct all payments for this business partner to this address.

This allows to identify the payer based on the address he as paid to.

Optional: Replicating Receivables to SAP Digital Currency Hub

Receivables processing can be enhanced by replicating receivables to the hub. When an incoming payment drops in, it will reconcile the open receivable with these transactions. It applies a simple mechanism, determining the business partner by receive-to address (see above), and matching the receivable by amount, based on a FIFO principle. The benefit is enhanced note-to-payee information when the transaction is sent to the ERP solution, allowing instant clearing in accounts receivable.

Summary

Stablecoin transactions are fast, taking only seconds or minutes. They can be used to pay your suppliers, without modification of your ERP system. You only need to set-up a dedicated house bank account and a payment method, and to integrate with SAP Digital Currency Hub. Then, existing means like payment runs are used to trigger a payment, and bank statements received from the hub are used to receive payments. The hub itself does not need your attention, only at setup.
